message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/
**Title:** Training of Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Induces Functional Reorganization in Late-Blind Humans (Frontiers / PMC)
**Found via:** time-scale modification algorithms search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract claims about ultra-fast speech comprehension: the playback speeds (in words per minute or syllables per second) that blind people can comprehend, the perceptual limits for sighted vs blind listeners, training/adaptation effects, time-compression methods used, and any neural/functional reorganization findings. Quote exact sentences with numbers where possible. Also note the publication date and journal.

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Claims

**Publication:** Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, October 23, 2013

## Playback Speeds

**Blind listeners' capabilities:**
- "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"
- Study used 18 syl/s as the "ultra-fast" test rate
- Four of six trained participants achieved approximately 70% comprehension at 18 syl/s

**Sighted listeners' limits:**
- "the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"
- One normally-sighted participant "reported that she had 'reached her limits'"

## Training Effects

The study found that after approximately 6 months of daily training using screen-reader software (JAWS text-to-speech):
- Four participants showed "considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)"
- Performance on moderately fast speech (8 syl/s) improved to mean 97% accuracy
- Accelerated speech at 13 syl/s reached mean 93% accuracy

## Neural Reorganization

Key finding: Blind participants with very low visual acuity recruited primary visual cortex (V1) and pulvinar for speech processing, while those with residual vision showed redistribution within frontal-temporal language networks.

content.text: Let me verify the key numbers and quotes directly from the page to ensure accuracy.

input.prompt: Find and quote verbatim sentences that mention: (1) the syllables-per-second rate that blind people can understand (around 22 syl/s) and the comparison to normal speaking rate (around 6 syl/s) and sighted untrained limit (8 syl/s); (2) the role of right fusiform gyrus or visual cortex (V1) in ultra-fast speech comprehension; (3) any statement about the time-compression / acceleration method used. Provide exact quotes.

content.content:
# Key Findings from the Research Article

## (1) Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Rates

**Blind individuals' capability:**
> "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"

**Comparison to sighted listeners:**
> "exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s"

## (2) Role of Visual Cortex in Ultra-Fast Speech

**Primary visual cortex (V1) involvement:**
> "blind people highly skilled in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar"

**Functional reorganization mechanism:**
> "the visual system seems to be recruited as a means to enhance actual temporal resolution of the acoustic signal via implementation of an extra-channel conveying information on syllabic structure"

## (3) Time-Compression Method

**Training procedure:**
> "the participants were instructed to use the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence')" and could "speed up more and more the syllable rate according to their actual training level"

content.text: I have the key claims with verbatim quotes. This is a peer-reviewed primary research article in Frontiers in Human Neuroscience (published via PMC).

claims.claim: Trained blind/vision-impaired individuals can learn to comprehend accelerated (time-compressed) speech at rates up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)

claims.claim: The upper comprehension limit for normal sighted listeners is about 8 syllables per second, far below the ~22 syl/s achieved by trained blind listeners, establishing a perceptual ceiling for untrained accelerated-speech comprehension.
claims.quote: exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s

claims.claim: Months of daily training (via a text-to-speech screen reader) produces measurable adaptation: four of six trained blind participants reached ~70% word comprehension at an ultra-fast 18 syl/s rate.
claims.quote: considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in skilled blind listeners is supported by functional reorganization recruiting the right primary visual cortex (V1), fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar, suggesting the visual system is repurposed to boost temporal/syllabic resolution of the acoustic signal.
claims.quote: the visual system seems to be recruited as a means to enhance actual temporal resolution of the acoustic signal via implementation of an extra-channel conveying information on syllabic structure

claims.claim: The accelerated speech used for training and testing was generated with a text-to-speech screen reader (JAWS, 'Eloquence' synthesizer), with syllable rate progressively sped up to match the listener's training level.
claims.quote: the participants were instructed to use the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence')

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from the peer-reviewed primary research article (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013-10-23, hosted on PMC). The source is highly relevant to the "speed listening" portion of the research question, establishing concrete perceptual limits (sighted ceiling ~8 syl/s vs. trained-blind ceiling ~22 syl/s), training/adaptation effects (~70% comprehension at 18 syl/s after ~6 months), the neural reorganization mechanism (recruitment of visual cortex/V1, fusiform gyrus, pulvinar), and the time-compression method (JAWS TTS with progressively increased syllable rate).
